sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: Let me check if the block moved this time.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
for n in current_frame.segmentation['nodes']:
    if n['color'] not in ['B', 'W', 'c', 'G']:
        center = (sum(p[0] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']),
                  sum(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])/len(n['boundary']))
        if center[0] > 5:
            print(f"Node: color={n['color']}, pixels={n['pixels']}, center~({center[0]:.0f},{center[1]:.0f})")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The block at (36, 18) moved to (34, 18)! So blocks CAN be moved, but only when I click close to them. The block moved 2 rows up from (36, 18) to (34, 18).
So the mechanic is: clicking at a position moves the nearest block towards that position by a small amount (2 rows/columns).
This explains why clicking at (30, 25) didn't work earlier - it was too far from any block. The blocks only move when the click is close to them.
Now I understand: I need to click near each block to move it incrementally. The block moves 2 pixels in the direction of the click.
So to move a block, I need to click at a position that's close to the block (within about 2-4 pixels).
Now the question is: what's the goal? Looking at the initial state, I moved a purple block along a diagonal path. The goal might be to move all blocks to specific positions.
But I triggered game-over when the purple block got too close to the blob. So the goal is NOT to move blocks into the blob.
Looking at the magenta blocks' positions: (34, 18), (40, 36), (56, 46), (56, 16). These are scattered around the blob at (27, 33).
Maybe the goal is to move all blocks to form a specific pattern, like a circle or square around the blob.
Actually, let me reconsider the game-over condition. When I moved the purple block to (19, 44), it was close to the blob at (15, 48). The distance was about sqrt(4^2 + 4^2) ≈ 5.7 pixels. This triggered game-over.
But wait, the blob was at (15, 48) initially, and after game-over it moved to (27, 33). This is a significant change. Maybe the game-over caused the level to reset with a new configuration.
Let me focus on what I can do now: move the magenta blocks. I need to figure out the target positions.
Looking at the initial state from the very first frame, there were blocks at various positions. But I don't have access to that anymore.
Let me try a different approach: move all blocks towards the blob and see what happens. If moving blocks too close triggers game-over, I'll know the goal is NOT to absorb blocks. If moving blocks to the blob completes the level, then that's the goal.
Let me move the block at (34, 18) towards the blob at (27, 33). The direction is roughly up and right. I'll click at (32, 20) which is 2 rows up and 2 columns right from the block.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Move block at (34, 18) towards blob: click at (32, 20)
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 32, 'col': 20}])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
